coredata.dc:title: Developing a label-free full-range highly selective pH nanobiosensor using a novel high quantum yield pH-responsive activated-protein-protected gold nanocluster prepared by a novel ultrasonication-protein-assisted procedure

coredata.dc:description: A novel, label-free, ultra-selective, reproducible, and reversible pH nanobiosensor was developed for analyzing biofluids, food samples, and real water media utilizing a novel activated-protein-protected gold nanocluster with an ultra-narrow emission band, termed as ABSA-AuNCs. The ABSA-AuNCs were synthesized via a novel ultrasonication-protein-assisted procedure, for the first time, using activated bovine serum albumin as both capping and reducing agents. The ABSA-AuNCs revealed a highly narrow symmetric emission spectrum (λmax = 330.0 nm upon excitation at 312–317 nm), and a highly narrow size distribution of 2.9–3.7 nm along with an enhanced quantum yield of 28.3 %. At present, with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 14.0 nm, ABSA-AuNCs have the narrowest bandwidth of fluorescent nanomaterials reported to date. The ABSA-AuNCs were characterized for their stability, size, morphology, crystallinity, structural, and optical properties. The ABSA-AuNCs were found to be appropriate for constructing a label-free ultraselective pH nanobiosensor. A linear range over 2.0–11.0, fast response time of less than 5 s, and long-term stability of 99.7 % after 500 min were achieved. The %RSD for repeatability, intra-day reproducibility, and inter-day reproducibility was found to be 1.4 %, 1.7 %, and 2.3 %, in order, to reveal high repeatable and reproducible results. The selectivity of the pH biosensor was evaluated upon the addition of different interferents, indicating an excellent pH selectivity for the ABSA-AuNCs. Real sample analysis proved the feasibility of the ABSA-AuNCs for accurate, precise, and reliable pH sensing in biofluids (undiluted blood and urine), a variety of food samples, and several real water samples.

head.ce:title: Developing a label-free full-range highly selective pH nanobiosensor using a novel high quantum yield pH-responsive activated-protein-protected gold nanocluster prepared by a novel ultrasonication-protein-assisted procedure

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hormozi Laboratory of Chemistry and Biochemistry, 9861334367, Zabol, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Hormozi Laboratory of Chemistry and Biochemistry

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hormozi Laboratory of Chemistry and Biochemistry, Baluchestan 9861334367, Zabol, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel, label-free, ultra-selective, reproducible, and reversible pH nanobiosensor was developed for analyzing biofluids, food samples, and real water media utilizing a novel activated-protein-protected gold nanocluster with an ultra-narrow emission band, termed as ABSA-AuNCs. The ABSA-AuNCs were synthesized via a novel ultrasonication-protein-assisted procedure, for the first time, using activated bovine serum albumin as both capping and reducing agents. The ABSA-AuNCs revealed a highly narrow symmetric emission spectrum (λmax = 330.0 nm upon excitation at 312–317 nm), and a highly narrow size distribution of 2.9–3.7 nm along with an enhanced quantum yield of 28.3 %. At present, with a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 14.0 nm, ABSA-AuNCs have the narrowest bandwidth of fluorescent nanomaterials reported to date. The ABSA-AuNCs were characterized for their stability, size, morphology, crystallinity, structural, and optical properties. The ABSA-AuNCs were found to be appropriate for constructing a label-free ultraselective pH nanobiosensor. A linear range over 2.0–11.0, fast response time of less than 5 s, and long-term stability of 99.7 % after 500 min were achieved. The %RSD for repeatability, intra-day reproducibility, and inter-day reproducibility was found to be 1.4 %, 1.7 %, and 2.3 %, in order, to reveal high repeatable and reproducible results. The selectivity of the pH biosensor was evaluated upon the addition of different interferents, indicating an excellent pH selectivity for the ABSA-AuNCs. Real sample analysis proved the feasibility of the ABSA-AuNCs for accurate, precise, and reliable pH sensing in biofluids (undiluted blood and urine), a variety of food samples, and several real water samples.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: pH plays a key role in a wide variety of environmental, industrial, life, and biomedical applications [1–3]. The pH value of cells affects cell growth, drug resistance, enzyme and tissue activities, signal transduction, and generation of cellular energy [4]. Moreover, the abnormal levels of pH cause several clinical problems, e.g., Alzheimer's [5] and cancer [6]. The blood pH is the most important acid-base parameter for cellular function and plays an important role in the diagnosis of chronic kidney disease via metabolic acidosis [7]. In addition, the acidic urine pH is associated with impaired renal function in primary gout patients [8]. In the environment, the pH value of freshwater, seawater, and soils can affect the nutrient availability, equilibria of several inorganic compounds, microorganism activity, and metal solubility [9]. Besides, in the food industry, pH measurements are a very important topic. Considering the above facts, the rapid, reliable, and accurate determination of pH values of aqueous solutions such as real water samples, biofluids, and food samples is an attractive research field for both clinical and environmental applications [10,11], as well as for quality checking of foods [12,13]. The most common approach for pH sensing is using the conventional potentiometers. The main drawbacks of this method are the temporal behavior of pH electrodes, requiring frequent calibration, limited long-term stability, and cleaning of the glass frit forming [9]. Besides, other methods, for instance, electrochemical methods [14], Raman-based sensors [15], colorimetric methods [16], and optical pH sensors including luminescence assays [17], fluorescence [11,18,19], and spectrophotometric probes [20,21] have been reported for pH quantification. The main principle of optical pH sensing is the change in the emission/absorbance of the probe as a function of pH variation [16,18–20,22]. Among them, the fluorescence pH probes such as carbon dots [4,23], FITC-dextran (500 kDa) loaded Ca-alginate microspheres [24], rhodamine [25], quinoline [5], indoles [26], carbon dots [27], coumarin [28], benzothiazole [29], dansyl-8-aminoquinoline [30], quaternary ammonium salt [31], and cyanine dyes [32] reveal high potential applications for pH measurements due to their low-cost, excellent pH selectivity, and low electrical interferences. However, most of the reported pH nanosensors work over neutral and slightly acidic conditions, and cannot operate harsh acidic media or over the full pH range [5] and most of the reported sensors did not provide any investigations about their selectivity or suffer from low selectivity, as reported [9,23,33–35]. Besides, most of the developed fluorescent pH nanosensors are reported without application in real media [10,34] or are only applicable for practical application in one type of real samples, e.g., only pH detection in aqueous amine solutions [9], selected water media [36], foodstuffs [13], urine [8,24], or serum [37]. To address these difficulties, we aimed to develop a label-free ultraselective full-range pH nanobiosensor with applicability for pH sensing in different real samples for instance, real water media, biofluids, and different food samples. Recently, the fluorescent properties of gold nanoclusters (AuNCs) have been investigated and applied for most analytical studies [38,39]. Also, AuNCs have been used for the construction of several nanozyme-based sensors due to their enzyme-mimic role [40,41]. Moreover, recently, it has been shown that the modulation of resonance energy transfer (RET) between a fluorescent dye and citrate-capped gold nanoparticles can be utilized for the detection of proteins [42]. Hence, considering their wide applications, different methods have been developed for their synthesis, e.g., etching [43], microwave-assisted [44], photoreductive [45], and protein-assisted synthesis [46]. Among these methods, protein-assisted methods are more attractive due to their green properties [46]. Protein-protected AuNCs such as BSA-AuNCs reveal many advantages such as the green approach of synthesis, high water solubility, biocompatibility, also, the ease of further conjugation in comparison with other types of AuNCs [40,41]. Despite the significant advantages, the current protein-protected AuNCs such as denatured-BSA-AuNCs, and BSA-AuNCs show very low quantum yields in the range of 6–8 % [44,47–49]. Besides, traditional fluorophores have certain characteristics such as rapid photobleaching, narrow absorption, and broad emission spectra as well as low quantum yield that limit their practical applications [50]. To solve these problems, research on developing novel fluorophores with narrow bandwidth and high quantum yield is underway for practical applications in superresolution optoelectronic devices, lasers, and especially sensing and imaging [51]. Unfortunately, regarding the AuNCs, the reported ones show wide bandwidths and low quantum yield. Hence, the synthesis of a new generation of gold nanoclusters with a narrow emission band and wide absorption spectrum, along with high quantum yield via the eco-friendly green protocols and their application in biomedical and analytical fields is one of the most important research topics. Based on our best knowledge, there is no report on the protein-assisted ultrasonication synthesis procedure for the synthesis of gold nanoclusters or any other fluorescent/non-fluorescent nanomaterials. Therefore, in this work, we aimed to combine the advantages of protein-assisted and sonochemical methods to produce highly stable, high-quantum-yield gold nanoclusters. In this regard, initially, a novel activated-protein-protected gold nanocluster (termed ABSA-AuNCs), was synthesized via the novel ultrasonication-protein-assisted procedure, for the first time, using activated bovine serum albumin as both capping and reducing agents. Factors affecting the FL intensity of nanocluster, including sonication time and cycle period, mg of capping agent, and incubation time have optimized. Thereafter, the novel ABSA-AuNCs were utilized for constructing a novel, label-free, full-range, ultra-selective, highly stable, high reproducible, and reversible pH nanobiosensor for analyzing biofluids, food samples, and real water media.

The synthesis of the ABSA-AuNCs was performed via an ultrasonication-protein-assisted procedure. At optimal experimental conditions, 10.0 mL of HAuCl4 (5.0 mM) was incubated at 37.0 ± 0.1 °C for 10.0 min. After that, 250.0 mg BSA was quickly added to the solution, following an optimized sonication procedure for the activation of BSA (ABSA). The sonication was performed by 20 sec, on and 5 sec, off for a total sonication time of 480 sec (On) for completion of the capping activation and capping-gold interaction. Separately, 1.0 mL NaOH (1.0 mM) was incubated at 37.0 ± 0.1 °C and then quickly added to the mixture of gold and activated BSA for preparing a strongly alkaline medium (pH, 9.5–11.0), suitable for the reduction process. Then, the resulting yellow–brown solution was incubated at 37.0 ± 0.1 °C under mild stirring for 16.0 hrs. The resulting brownish-red ABSA-AuNCs were then collected and stored at 4.0 °C in the dark. The pink-emitted ABSA-AuNCs have a symmetric emission band over 310–350 nm with a maximum wavelength of 330.0 nm upon excitation at 312.0–317.0 nm.

ce:section.ce:section-title: pH biosensing and validation of pH nanobiosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Regarding pH biosensing, 30.0 µL of the ABSA-AuNCs (as the pH analytical probe) was added to 200.0 µL of universal buffer solution (50 mM) with different pH values over 2.0–11.0 and then the mixture was diluted to 2.0 mL by DI water. Then, the FL spectra of the introduced ABSA-AuNCs were measured and the calibration curve was constructed by plotting the FL intensity at 330.0 nm as the function of pH. For the validation of the pH nanobiosensor, the figure of the merits of the sensor including response time, response stability, and reversibility were evaluated for both acidic and basic conditions. In this regard, two separate phosphate buffer solutions with pH=4.0 or 8.0 were prepared and then 30.0 µL of ABSA-AuNCs was exposed to these solutions (final volume = 2.0 mL). The analytical signal was probed both immediately after the addition of ABSA-AuNCs to the sample solution and in different time intervals from 5 s to 120 s for the response time investigation and in a period over 10.0 min-500.0 min for the response stability tests. Moreover, the reversibility of the introduced nanobiosensor was investigated upon pH adjusting from 4.0 to 8.0, and vice versa.

The pH selectivity of the nanobiosensor was investigated through the pH determination of a phosphate buffer (0.05 M, pH=7.2) in the presence of different possible co-existing interferents including uric acid (UA), KCl, NaCl, glucose, Fe2+, Cu2+, Cl-, NO3 –, and some amino acids such as L-valine (L-Val), L-glutamic acid (L-Glu), and L-aspartic acid (L-Asp). It should be noted that the pH recovery (%) and the relative errors (RE) for the pH quantification in the presence of interfering compounds were estimated using the following formulas;

Experimental details of real sample analysis including biofluids (urine and blood), water media (tap, river, pool, and packed water samples), and foodstuffs (black tea, ice tea, malt, fresh orange juices, and fresh lemon juices) using the introduced ABSA-AuNCs are provided in SI file, section S3.

The activation of the BSA protein was done using ultrasonication-based methods. It must be noted that the activation of BSA is the most important step affecting the optical behavior of the synthesized ABSA-AuNCs. Hence, the activation process was optimized via evaluation of the effect of sonication time (Fig. 1 A) and sonication cycle period (Fig. 1B) on the FL intensity and optical spectra shape, showed that the FL properties of ABSA-AuNCs are at their best value when the activation was done for about 480.0 s along with a sonication cycle period over 10.0–20.0 s (more details in SI file, section S4). Besides, the initial amount of BSA (Fig. 1C) and incubation time (Fig. 1D) as two effective factors on the optical properties of ABSA-AuNCs were optimized. Results revealed the best optical properties of ABSA-AuNCs upon using 250.0 mg of BSA within 16.0 hrs incubation (details in section S4, SI file).

The optical properties of ABSA-AuNCs, including PL and absorption behaviors, storestability, reproducibility, optical band gaps, and quantum yield were evaluated by UV–Visible and FL spectroscopy. The UV–visible spectra of bovine serum albumin (BSA), activated bovine serum albumin (ABSA), and ABSA-AuNCs are shown in Fig. 2 A, the presence of the characteristic peak of protein (280.0 nm) in the ABSA spectrum points that the protein structure saved during the activation process. The ABSA showed a new peak around 350.0 nm which is related to electron transitions of the C=O groups of the amides present in the protein structure [52–54] while these transitions did not occur in the non-activated BSA, indicating successful activation of the capping agent. Regarding the ABSA-AuNCs, the results indicated the conjugation of ABSA to gold cores in the ABSA-AuNCs structure (details in S5, SI file). The optical band gaps (Eg) of the ABSA (Fig. 2B), and ABSA-AuNCs (Fig. 2C) were investigated, revealing a bandgap of 1.57 eV for ABSA and 1.30 eV for ABSA-AuNCs, revealing improvement of the bandgap of ABSA about 0.27 eV upon its conjugation with gold atoms in ABSA-AuNCs [55] (more details in S5, SI file).

Fig. 3 A shows the excitation and emission peaks of the ABSA-AuNCs, their photograph under UV-lamp (365 nm), as well as the excitation and emission peaks of BSA and ABSA. Both BSA and ABSA showed a symmetric FL spectrum centered at 350.0 nm upon excitation at 338.0 nm which the peak of BSA is close to its previously reported FL spectrum [56]. As seen, both spectra shape and emission λmax of BSA did not change after its activation via the sonication method, indicating that no considerable changes in protein structure have occurred during the activation, however, the FL intensity of ABSA is higher than this non-activated form. Regarding brownish-red ABSA-AuNCs, the as-synthesized pink emitted ABSA-AuNCs, show a symmetric excitation peak. As seen in Fig. 3, the ABSA-AuNCs revealed a symmetric and highly narrow emission spectrum over 314.0–344.0 nm with a λmax of 330.0 nm and a full width at half maximum (FWHM) of 14.0 nm upon excitation at 312.0 nm. Notably, one may assume that the FL spectrum of the ABSA-AuNCs centered at 330 nm excitation at 312 nm might be ascribed to the ABSA. Hence, the FL spectrum of the BSA and ABSA upon excitation at 312 nm (not 338 nm, 388 nm was the best λmax of BSA and ABSA) was also supplemented to confirm the FL performance of the product. The results shown in Fig. 3B revealed that upon excitation at 312 nm, ABSA and BSA showed an FL spectrum with a λmax at 348 nm and their FL intensity at 330 nm was negligible (around zero), proving the FL performance of the ABSA-AuNCs. Besides, the effect of the excitation wavelength on the FL behavior of the as-prepared ABSA-AuNCs was also checked by recording the FL spectra of the introduced ABSA-AuNCs upon excitation at different excitation wavelengths over 290–325 nm (Fig. 3C). The results revealed that the maximal FL intensity was observed at 312 nm and it was approximately stabled over 312–317 nm and then the intensity was decreased by increasing the excitation wavelength. The reproducibility of the fluorescence properties of the introduced ABSA-AuNCs was evaluated, in Fig. 3D, revealing an RSD percentage of less than 1.0 %. The storage stability (shelf-life) of the ABSA-AuNCs was investigated (Fig. S6), and showed excellent stability for the ABSA-AuNCs (saving 94.5 % and 74.3 % of its initial intensity after 10 and 40 days, respectively (details in section S6). The PL quantum yield (QY) of the introduced ABSA-AuNCs was also estimated (details in section S7), revealing an absolute quantum yield was calculated at about 0.283 (%QY=28.3 %). The QY of conventional protein-protected AuNCs is very low over 6–8 % [47–49] and the QY of the other reported AuNCs is variable from 1.2 %-24.3 % as previously reported in the literature [57–68]. Hence, the quantum yield of the gold nanoclusters was significantly enhanced upon their synthesis via the developed combined protein-assisted- ultrasonication procedure.

The Fourier-transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra of the BSA, ABSA, and ABSA-AuNCs were recorded to prove the successful synthesis of the ABSA-AuNCs (Fig. 4 A–B). Based on the FT-IR results, the gold atoms are coordinated to the activated BSA via nitrogen atoms of amide (i.e. C-N bond), −C=O, –NH, and –OH groups presented in the peptide backbone, and C-O of carboxylic acid presented in the protein structure (details in SI file, section S8). It should be mentioned that for conventional protein-protected gold nanoclusters, the conjugation of the native protein and gold atoms is only performed via the –NH and –OH groups as reported in the literature [48,56,69]. Hence, it can be concluded that the ABSA provides higher coordination sites for gold atoms than the native BSA, showing the importance of the activation step. It must be noted that the Au atoms interact with sulfurs of ABSA as the other origin of the nanocluster protection (for more detail please see the EDX data) [56,69–73]. Besides, based on the FT-IR spectrum [74], the degree of crystallinity and relative crystallinity of the ABSA-AuNCs were estimated at about 0.475 and 47.5 %, respectively. The crystalline structure of ABSA-AuNCs was investigated by recording its X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern over 5-70° (Fig. 4C). The peaks positioned at 11.76, 29.47, 45.7, and 62.00° were found to be the characteristic peaks of ABSA-AuNCs, related to the coordinated protein [56], (111), (200), and (220) plans of gold as reported [75–77]. Moreover, the XRD pattern was used for crystalline size estimation [78], revealing a crystalline size of 5.0 nm. The relative crystallinity of the ABSA-AuNCs was also estimated at 50.0 % which are closed to FT-IR analysis (47.5 %) (Details of XRD in section S9, SI). The empirical formula and elemental composition of ABSA-AuNCs were evaluated using energy dispersive X-ray (EDX) analysis (Fig. 4D). The EDX analysis revealed the successful formation of ABSA-AuNCs along with an empirical formula of Au2.55SC216.10N90.10O98.10.2NaCl of estimated from the semi-quantitative (Table S1) (details in section S10, SI). Afterward, the introduced ABSA-AuNCs nanoclusters were characterized for their size and morphology using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), and dynamic light scattering (DLS) analysis. The TEM image of the ABSA-AuNCs revealed that the nanoclusters have uniform and spherical particles with a highly arrow size distribution over 2.9–3.7 nm with an average of 3.4 nm (Fig. 4A). Moreover, the mean size of the ABSA-AuNCs was calculated about 4.9 ± 1.1 nm based on DLS analysis (Fig. 4B). The results obtained from DLS (4.9 ± 1.1 nm) are in good agreement with the results of TEM imaging (3.4 nm) and XRD analysis (5.0 nm). After careful characterization of ABSA-AuNCs, to more accurately differentiate between the introduced ABSA-AuNCs and the conventional protein-protected gold nanoclusters, the conventional BSA-AuNCs were synthesized based on the Xie et al. method [47] and their optical properties were investigated and comprised with those of the introduced ABSA-AuNCs (Fig. S2–S4), revealing very better performances for ABSA-AuNCs than traditional BSA-AuNCs (details in SI file, S11). Besides, a typical comparison between the figure of merits of the introduced AuNCs and reported gold nanoclusters was performed and the results are shown in Table S2, revealing very better figure of merits of ABSA-AuNCs compared of reported ones.

To prove the applicability of the introduced ABSA-AuNCs for pH sensing in different media, the photostability of ABSA-AuNCs in different pHs was evaluated according to the method reported by Guo et al. [23]. In this regard, the fluorescence intensity of the as-prepared ABSA-AuNCs with different pHs (pH=2.0, 6.0, 9.0) was measured as a function of time (over 0.0–100 min) and the stability of the FL intensity was used as an index for photostability of the nanoprobe. The results shown in Fig. S5 revealed that the introduced ABSA-AuNCs hold ultra-photostability at different pH (pH=2.0, 6.0, 9.0). Hence, it can be concluded that ABSA-AuNCs hold ultra-photostability at different pHs and are suitable for reliable pH nanobiosensing in real conditions. Afterward, as it is well-known to develop a pH nanobiosensor using the introduced ABSA-AuNCs, it was necessary to evaluate the pH-sensitive response time for the system. Hence, the response time of the pH-sensitive system of ABSA-AuNCs was investigated at acidic (pH=4.0) and alkaline (pH=8.0) pHs in a phosphate buffer solution (0.05 M). The results showed that the FL intensity of the ABSA-AuNCs was immediately (less than 5 s) changed upon pH variation and then remained stable for a long time (Fig. S6). The highly short response time of the pH-sensitive fluorescence system of ABSA-AuNCs is a significant advantage of the presented nanobiosensor for rapid pH biosensing in real media.

The figures of merit of the developed nanosensor including, linear working range, calibration sensitivity, long-term stability, response stability, reversibility, repeatability, reproducibility, and accuracy were evaluated. The corresponding FL spectra of the ABSA-AuNCs are shown in Fig. 5 A, as seen, the FL intensity of ABSA-AuNCs was significantly quenched by increasing pH from 2.0 to 11.0. As a significant advantage, the change in FL intensity of ABSA-AuNCs at 330.0 nm shows a linear relationship with pH variations. Hence, the calibration plot for pH quantification was constructed by plotting the FL intensity of the as-prepared pH-sensitive nanoprobe as a function of pH variations of the media, providing a full-range linear working range over pH=2.0–11.0. The calibration sensitivity as one of the most important figures of merit of a sensor from a practical point of view was also calculated from the slope of the calibration plot, revealing a very high calibration sensitivity of 84.354 for the developed pH quantification method (Fig. 5B). The long-term stability of the introduced pH nanobiosensor was evaluated in different pH values (4.0, and 8.0) over the different time intervals between 0.0–500.0 min (Fig. 5C) at ambient temperature upon storage of solutions in the open air and daylight to prove its real stability for pH sensing. The long-term stability percentage was calculated using the following formula;

The results are shown in Fig. 5C, revealing response stability of 99.4 % at acidic pHs and 99.7 % at alkaline media after 500 min (about 8.3 hrs) of the first pH record. The results revealed high long-term stability for the presented pH nanobiosensor which is suitable for reliable pH measurements without any significant effect of light and air at a certain pH value. Moreover, the reversibility of the introduced nanobiosensor was investigated upon pH adjusting from 4.0 to 8.0, and vice versa via recording FL spectra (Fig. S7). The FL response vs. cycle index was plotted (Fig. 5D), showing excellent reversibility between the acidic and alkaline media. The repeatability of the nanobiosensor was also evaluated for both acidic (pH=4.0) and basic (pH=8.0) pHs, Fig. S8, revealing a %RSD of 1.5 % (n = 5) and 1.4 % (n = 5) for successive measurements in acidic and basic pHs, in order. It is well-known that a sensor lacking acceptable reproducibility is not applicable for practical uses, hence, the reproducibility of the introduced nanobiosensor was evaluated by intra-day (Fig. S9A) and inter-day (Fig. S9B) assays for pH=4.0 (acidic) and pH=8.0 (basic). Regarding intra-day assay, a %RSD as low as 2.0 % and 1.7 % was achieved for the pH detection in acidic (pH=4.0) and basic (pH=8.0) media, in order. Besides, an inter-day %RSD of 2.3 % and 2.4 % was obtained for the pH quantification in acidic and basic media, in turn. The accuracy of the results of the introduced pH nanobiosensor was evaluated by analyzing the commercial standardized buffered pH solutions (pH=4.00 ± 0.01, 7.00 ± 0.01, 10.00 ± 0.01) provided by Merck Company which were used for calibration of commercial pH meters. The results are shown in Table S3. The results showed that the introduced nanobiosensor can accurately detect the pH of commercial solutions with an accuracy percentage as high as 99.3–101.2 %. Notably, the pH of the standardized buffered pH solutions was also calculated by the commercial pH meter, the accuracy percentage of the commercial pH meter was found to be over 99.1–103.7 %. Notably, the accuracy percentage of the developed method is highly close to the accuracy percentage of the commercial pH meter. According to these results, it can be concluded that the introduced ABSA-AuNCs can utilized as accurate and reliable pH probes (indicator).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity of the developed pH nanobiosensor
ce:section.ce:para: The pH selectivity of the developed nanobiosensor was investigated via the pH determination of a 0.05 M phosphate buffer (pH=7.2) in the presence of different possible co-existing interferents including uric acid (UA; 100 mg/L), KCl (1000 mg/L), NaCl (1000 mg/L), glucose (200 mg/L), some cations and anions such as Fe2+ (40 mg/L), Cu2+ (40 mg/L), Cl−1 (1000 mg/L), and NO3 – (40 mg/L) and some amino acids such as L-valine (L-Val; 360 mg/L), L-glutamic acid (L-Glu; 550 mg/L), and L-aspartic acid (L-Asp; 900 mg/L). The corresponding FL spectra are shown in Fig. 5E, as seen, different interfering compounds have no considerable effect on the FL intensity of the introduced ABSA-AuNCs. Besides, the plot of pH response vs. interferents, Fig. 5F, revealed a pH recovery as high as 98.7–101.1 % in the presence of the co-existing interferents revealing excellent selectivity of the developed nanobiosensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Practical application of the developed pH nanobiosensor
ce:section.ce:para: Initially, The ABSA-AuNCs were utilized for pH biosensing in biofluids (human blood serum and urine) without any dilution or deproteinization (Table 1 ), indicating high pH recoveries over 98.6–102.7 % in blood serum samples and very excellent recoveries between 98.3–102.4 % for urine. It is notable that, the results of the as-prepared sensor are constituted to the results obtained by the standard commercial pH meter. Afterward, to prove the applicability of ABSA-AuNCs for practical analysis of the real water media, Tap water (Zabol, Iran), pool water, river water, and packed drinking water (Vanitar drinking water, Parsian Kavir Co., Iran) were analyzed (Table 1), indicating that high pH recoveries over 98.5–101.5 % for different water samples. The results of the as-prepared sensor are significantly constituted of those obtained by the standard method. Finally, several food samples including black tea, ice tea, malt, fresh orange juices, and fresh lemon juices were analyzed by both developed and standard methods (Table 1), the results of the proposed method are completely close to those obtained by the standard method and excellent pH recoveries over 99.2–103.5 % were achieved for different foodstuffs. Considering the results of this investigation, it can be deduced that the developed method is applicable for practical application in a wide range of real samples and is applicable in the real world.

A novel, label-free, full-range, ultra-selective, stable, reproducible, and reversible pH nanobiosensor was developed for analyzing biofluids, food samples, and real water media utilizing a novel activated-protein protected gold nanocluster with ultra-narrow emission band. The activated-protein-protected gold nanocluster (termed ABSA-AuNCs) with better characteristics than the reported AuNCs, was synthesized via a combined ultrasonication-protein-assisted procedure, for the first time. The ABSA-AuNCs revealed a highly narrow and symmetric emission spectrum along with an enhanced PL QY=28.3 %. At present, with an FWHM of 14.0 nm, the ABSA-AuNCs have the narrowest bandwidth of fluorescent nanomaterials reported to date. The ABSA-AuNCs were found to be appropriate for constructing a label-free ultraselective pH nanobiosensor. A linear range over 2.0–11.0, fast response time of less than 5 s, and long-term stability of 99.7 % after 500 min were achieved. Real sample analysis proved the feasibility of the ABSA-AuNCs for accurate, precise, and reliable pH sensing in biofluids (undiluted blood and urine), a variety of food samples, and several real water samples. However, despite the significant advantages of the developed sensor, its application for in vivo detection and red blood analysis is still on limitation. Notably, the figures of merit of the developed pH nanobiosensor were compared to the reported ones (Table S4), revealing very better figures of merit for the developed nanobiosensor than the reported ones [6,79–81].
Saeed Reza Hormozi Jangi: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization.
